speaker: interviewee
text: One significant event that took place when I was in Auschwitz, I had a very good friend of mine who's name is Amek Birnbaum. We both went to this school in the ghetto. I knew him from before the war. I knew his mother, I knew his brother. His brother, Davidek, was about nine years old at the time. He was the only survivor, young survivor at Blizyn. It is a long story of how he survived in 1943 as a nine-year-old, but he did survive. And he did go with us to Auschwitz in 1944. And when we arrived at Auschwitz, because we were mainly young people and we were already dressed in the striped clothing of prisoners, there was no selection on the ramp. So Davidek passed right into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: However, selections took place periodically inside the camp. And on one of those days, Davidek's number was written down to be selected and sent to the gas chambers because he was a youngster. He was with his brother. Across the camp was their mother, Salka. And Salka knew the score. And she was always apprehensive as to what's going to happen to her young Davidek. And she always kept inquiring about him, you know, how is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, after Davidek was selected, the way these selections took place-- let me just briefly describe this to you-- is a German officer would come in dressed in a white coat. Why he dressed in a white coat, as if to imply that he was some kind of a doctor, and that he's going to put you through some kind of an examination. But basically, the-- the white frock and the officer meant simply to select those he considered too weak to work for the gas chambers. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one day at such selection, he selected Davidek. His number was written down, and he was bothered at the time. You know, when they had a room, and when they had the opportunity, they would send a truck to collect all these people who had been selected and take them to the crematorium at a given day. Davidek the nine-year-old had been selected one day, but he remained in camp. And Salka, who was nearby, didn't know about it, of course, nobody told her. Well, after an interval of about 10 days, trucks came to collect those that were marked for the selection. And of course, Davidek in our barrack was one of those selected. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When these people who were selected were about to board the trucks, to avoid-- they were so efficient, these Germans. They gave them a quarter of a bread with a smear of marmalade on the bread to make them eager to jump up on the truck. Do you understand? So there was a-- there was a cart in front of the truck. And as these people were checked out, their numbers were checked out. Before they jumped up on the truck, they would get a quarter of a loaf of bread with a smear of marmalade on it. And they would eagerly jump up on the truck and begin to eat with the greatest frenzy, because there was-- everybody was so hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Davidek had gotten this quarter of a loaf of bread with the marmalade, and I witnessed this myself, and he-- he was nine years old. He ate the marmalade off the bread, but he didn't eat the bread. When the time came and the trucks revved up and moved out, he threw the quarter of a loaf of bread to his brother, Amek. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And he said, where I go, I won't need this. Now, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the mother, Salka, got never got over that. She never was able to admit to herself that she lost this Davidek. She is now a woman of 92 or 93, and I speak to her occasionally. Just the other day she told me that she's tired of living. She would like to join her husband, who had died about 10 years ago. She also said that she spoke to a rabbi, and she asked the rabbi's permission to take into her grave [SOBBING] Davidek's picture. She just told me that a couple of days ago. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I think I want to continue with the post-Auschwitz life now. In November, we were evacuated from Auschwitz to a place called Lieberose, which was nothing but a holding pen for German industry to pick out slave laborers. It was a terrible camp. People were dying by the hundreds every day, just simply of starvation. No food. No food. Just-- and one day a couple of German officers came on the scene, and they asked for 60 machine mechanics. They needed 60 machine mechanics. And they needed 60, but since this was such a death camp and everybody, you know, was on the verge of dying of starvation, about 400 people moved out. They said they were all mechanics.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans didn't know what to do. So they decided they were going to give us a test, whether or not we're mechanics. But how are they going to give us a test if they had no machinery? They had nothing there. But being German, and being as efficient as they were, they were going to ask technical questions. OK? And that gave me the incentive to stay. After a few threats that they were going to shoot those who wouldn't pass, maybe 200 people remained for that 60-- 60 job place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my turn came, they asked me a couple questions that I learned in fourth and fifth grade, how to figure a circle, or some such thing, and I passed. Having passed, the 60 of us went to a place called Peenemunde, which is all the way north on the Baltic Sea, where the Germans were making manufacturing V2 rockets at the time. And out of the 60 Jews that came out of Peenemunde, I was one of two that survived that Peenemunde camp. And the reason I survived with the other young boy was because when we came to there, they asked if there were any juveniles, [NON-ENGLISH], among the people. And the two of us stepped out, and they put us into a room for juveniles. The others, there were no Jews on that-- on that camp. So our identities kind of got lost. And nobody knew, at that point, nobody knew that we were Jewish. The two of us were among 30 or 40 Polish and Russian boys, some as young as 10 years. And our hopes for survival soared, because we were always afraid that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even if we survive, on the last day they would kill the Jews anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't-- they didn't check at that point about circumcision and what have you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No, at that point, it didn't matter anymore, because this island never had Jews. And the only Jews that came were among the 60. And the two of us, when we joined this youthful group, nobody cared. Nobody asked us. Nobody knew. And we stayed on Peenemunde for about six weeks, I believe. And then Peenemunde was evacuated as well. And we shuttled back and forth across Germany for perhaps a month or so. On the 1st of April we arrived at a town called Bergen. And we took over a panzer army-- panzer unit camp that had been evacuated the day before. And we knew we were very close to liberation. And on April 15, I looked out a window, and I saw downstairs British Tommies, with a armored vehicle, five pointed allied star on it. And I just want to read the last, the ending of the story for you. On April 15, 1945, at 9:15 AM, I heard a commotion downstairs. I walked over to the open window to see what was happening. Daniel was embracing his-- Daniel was the other boy that survived with me in Peenemunde-- was embracing a soldier. A Russian boy was kneeling and kissing the hand of another soldier. One of these soldiers were dressed in khaki. And in the background was an armored vehicle with a five pointed white star on its hood. The Allies had arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: My knees buckled. I barely made it to my bunk. Resting my elbows on my knees, I held my head in my hands. I no longer cared about food. I knew I would never crawl again. Questions kept popping into my head. Who am I? What am I? Where am I? Is anyone left of my family? Will there be a life as I once knew it? Eventually, I pulled myself together and went downstairs. British soldiers were all over the camp dispensing crackers and a milk-like substance. They were fumigating people with DDT.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then from the administrative building, the Russians dragged out the body of an SSman they had beaten to death. I came closer and saw the grotesque-looking body still twitching, dressed in the hated SS uniform. Strangely, I found my need for revenge had already left me. At that moment, I wished for nothing more than a return to normalcy so I could find out if any members of my family were alive. It was as if part of nature which had steeled me for survival had now abandoned me. Different rules would now prevail. It was going to be a new world, a new beginning, a new life with different values. I was newly born. With my English I had learned at Ms Sobel's Clandestine School. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked the British officer about the state of the war. The Nazis kaput, was his answer. Those were the sweetest words I heard all my life. Have you liberated any Jews? Were any Jews left alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me that he had just come from a nearby concentration camp in a place called Belsen where there were many Jewish survivors. He then took me to his jeep and gave me several cans of conserved meat. Daniel had also gotten some food from the soldiers and we had a liberation feast. But our camp immunities no longer worked. And for the next two weeks, we were sick with dysentery, and spent most of the time in a makeshift hospital. The real shock of liberation came a few days later, after I came out of the hospital, when Polish survivors of Bergen-- when the Polish survivors of Bergen-Belsen arranged a liberation ball. I walked into the hall dressed in a jacket and slacks obtained from the Red Cross, feeling like a human being for the first time in years. The band was playing a song called [NON-ENGLISH], At that point, the memory of my experiences clashed with the unlimited possibilities of my future.

speaker: interviewer
text: My feet below the knees went numb. I grabbed a chair and barely managed to sit down. A pretty girl noticed and came over to me. Hi, my name is Rayna. Are you all right? I think so, I said, it's just that this is so much. She sat down next to me. I know, she said, I'm Jewish, too. I survived the war with my mother by pretending to be Polish Catholics. We worked on a farm not far from here. I thought we were the only Jews who made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the feeling. My name is Josef and I'm so very happy to meet you. I was still struggling to express my feelings when she threw her hands around my neck. Never mind that now. Come, let's dance. And so we danced that night. Oh, how we danced. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I want to take this opportunity to thank Mr. Spielberg and the-- his organization for making this interview possible. I know that all of us survivors have unique stores to tell, and many people right now may not be so interested in listening to it. So the opportunity to do that is greatly appreciated by me as well as as by all the others. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Introduce your family. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Of course, on my left is my beautiful wife, Ganka Gaina Dina Danushia Lastman Horn. And to my right is my middle daughter, Bonnie. Bonnie Judith was named after my sister, Bluma. And Sandra, Sandra Joy. Sandra Joy is named after my-- after my wife's mother, Sarah, and after my mother, Yohevet. And last but not least is my beautiful daughter Ellen, Ellen May. Ellen is named after my brother, Eli, and May is the name of--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's sister, Manya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anything that you would like to say about your parents doing this video, and about the way they raised you, and you being a survivor's child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to say that I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Take the mic and let her talk into it. It's easier that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want me to hook her up?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's OK. She can hold it in her hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can just hook it on your--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just right there, that's easier.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say that I have always felt very normal growing up, like everybody else, all the other kids. I've always felt very lucky to be in a family that was so full of love, and support, and warmth, and pride. And I remember when I first became aware of the fact that we were a little bit different than everyone else, I remember being very angry when someone said to me, well, your mother has an accent. And I felt very protective. And children, of course, want to be like everybody else. And as I became older, I realized that we weren't like everybody else. I remember being about 11 or 12 years old, and looking through a closet in our basement in our home in Brooklyn, and finding some memoirs that were written by my father about his experiences. And although I was aware of some of what they went through, it never really was a part of our lives in a vivid sense. It was a part of our lives, but not really what we focused on at all. We had family that we were close to. We had holidays we celebrated. We were a family. Not having grandparents wasn't anything that I really missed, because I never really had it to miss. And I didn't know what I was missing. So I never really felt that it was a terrible loss, especially having my parents, and their love, and aunts, and uncles that did survive. But I did lock myself in the bathroom, I remember, to read these memoirs, because I desperately craved more information about what my parents had gone through. And because they weren't all that verbal about talking about it, it was a way that they could communicate. The reason why I locked myself in the bathroom is because they were upset that I was going to be reading some of this. And I don't think they were really all that anxious for me to be exposed to some of the horrors at that particular age. And I think they were written soon after my father came to this country. Because I remember that his English wasn't as refined as it is today, as far as his grammar, and spelling, and so on. And that-- that was also very poignant for me. But in a way, reading those memoirs was a-- a door that opened and allowed me to question, and discuss, and-- and ask, and wonder about what had happened to them, and what they had gone through. And I always felt that my father was as open and direct in answering my questions as he could be. I think as I became older and asked more questions, he was more and more descriptive. I think meting out information that he thought that I could handle. My mother wasn't as verbal about it, but it was OK. And today, I have to say that I feel very special, and different. And I do take pride in my heritage, and pride in being a Jew, and a second generation survivor. I think that what it is that they went through is part of my legacy, and part of who I am, and very much a part of what I want to share with my own children, and have them share with their children, and so on. It is very much a part of who I am, and always will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very hard to summarize what it feels like-- what your own identity feels like in just a few minutes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think for me, my-- my first memory, my first understanding that my parents had survived the Holocaust, was the numbers on their skin, which I think frightened me. Maybe-- maybe didn't really frighten me, but I noticed it. I don't think that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was scared of it as much as it was a fact, an-- a fact that existed all the time. And the information about the numbers was given to us in such a way as not to frighten us, but to-- we learned of a sadness. And it became part of our lives, but it didn't rule our lives. And it didn't make us miserable, it just gave us another sense of a past that our parents had that really wasn't part of our lives so much, but was just a common, everyday fact that we had. I have always been very proud of my parents. I have never felt ashamed of accents or numbers. In fact, I have had a fantasy in some way that my parents are superior to other people because they survived. And I feel that they're very strong, and very wonderful, and that something about them contributed in some way to their ability to survive. And I feel stronger. I feel that because of their strength, that I am stronger, and our family is stronger. It's just a private feeling that I always have kept with me. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Even today-- I think possibly more today than ever-- I feel very proud of my parents. I feel very proud of the way they have assimilated into this country. I feel very proud of their successes. And for any things that they lack, I always-- I forgive them. I feel that they have managed most beautifully. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the fact that they have been able to become such vibrant and happy people, and such contributors to this world, is a true testament to them. I think, perhaps, one unusual thing about me and my understanding of what happened to my parents is that I have a very good memory. I remember most things. I rely on my memory. I don't make a lot of lists, and I really function very well in the things that I remember. However, it's quite remarkable to me that I have heard stories throughout my life about things that have happened to my parents, and yet many, many times, I feel as though the story is brand new, and that I have never heard it before. And I feel it's truly remarkable what-- what denial can be, that the story can be so poignant when I hear it, and then I have to forget it because it's really much too painful for me to really want to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that, for example, when I read the stories that my father has written, it's as though they were brand new and I had never heard them before. And even if my sisters refer to them, I can't remember them. And for somebody with a good memory, it's really quite unusual. And I just think it's because I don't like to think about the pain that they've had to go through. And I prefer to believe that it's gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was about five years old when I first began understanding that my heritage was different than many of my peers. And I knew that I-- unlike them, I didn't have grandparents, and I didn't have a very extended family. And from almost the moment I began understanding that-- that I had a different heritage, I begin my quest, really, for more information. I remember countless nights sitting at dinner. Dinner was always a very important time in our household. It was a time where we had all kinds of conversations about what had happened, political conversations, and historical conversations. And my father used to tell me stories about what had happened, and my mother slowly began telling me stories about what had happened. And I continued to want more information. I wanted to have an understanding of the broader historical context that all of this happened in. And I went to-- when I went to college, I began to study the Holocaust. And I did my thesis on American Zionists and their contribution to rescue efforts. And I would like to say thank you to everyone here today, because I think that-- that this is really all part of never again, and to express my appreciation for the people who made this possible. It's more important than anybody can understand. And I want to express my-- my thanks to my parents for doing what they did today, because it is so hard-- hard for them to do. But nobody will ever be able to forget or deny that the Holocaust happened ever again.
